model_summary: Initial gVAR-ready observation model targeting six causally meaningful, modifiable predictors (P01–P06) to salutogenically optimize state given the complaint’s six operationalized criteria (C01–C06). The criteria capture external emotional labor, help-seeking barriers, emotional suppression, delayed breakdowns, role fatigue from being “the strong one,” and boundary-setting skills. Predictors span psychosocial skills training (boundary setting, assertive help seeking, emotion expression, self-compassion, mindfulness), plus sleep/circadian regulation as a biological lever. The plan emphasizes frequent momentary sampling (4 EMA prompts/day) plus a light passive sleep signal, over a 14-day window to maximize within-person variance and feasibility for graphical VAR (gVAR) modeling. Tenable measurement scales (1–9 Likert where appropriate) and explicit lag assumptions support estimation without overfitting or redundancy.
variable_selection_notes: Rationale: The complaint centers on chronic boundary overreach, suppressed distress, and subsequent crashes. Six criteria were distilled to target both internal processes (emotional awareness, help-seeking, self-compassion) and external dynamics (boundary setting, role fatigue). Six predictors were chosen as core, modifiable levers with broad, plausible mechanistic links to multiple criteria, while keeping a compact, estimable design (minimizing collinearity and redundant self-regulation constructs). Sleep/circadian function (P05) is included as a robust biological regulator with strong links to affect regulation and energy, and mindfulness (P06) provides transdiagnostic regulation benefits. Bias/uncertainty: predictor effects are treated as potentially nonlinear for some pairs (e.g., boundary setting and social dynamics); signs may vary by individual, hence several edges are flagged as unknown/nonlinear where appropriate. Trade-offs: a compact, high-signal set reduces estimation risk while still capturing the complaint’s core salutogenic pathways.

momentary_ema.notes: Distribute prompts across morning/afternoon/evening to capture daily variation.

daily_diary.notes: Evening diary to summarize distress, boundary attempts, and perceived support.

passive_streams.notes: Sleep duration and basic sleep-wake signals from wearable/app; use daily aggregate to complement EMA.

design_recommendations.gvar_readiness_notes: Moderate; goal is stable sampling with acceptable missingness (<30%).
design_recommendations.variance_support_notes: Variables designed to maximize within-person variance (4 prompts/day; 1 sleep signal/day; 1 evening diary).
design_recommendations.missingness_risk_notes: Plan for automated reminders; imputation considered for <=25% missing per participant.

safety_notes: Not a diagnosis; no medication/dosing recommendations. Monitor for acute risk signals; escalate to care if self-harm risk emerges.

criteria_variables.label: Frequency of being sought for others' problems

measurement.item_or_signal: In the moment, how frequently are you approached by others to listen to or solve their problems today? (1-9, 1=rarely, 9=very often)
measurement.notes: Captures current external emotional labor load.

criteria_variables.mapped_leaf_full_path: NON-CLINICAL / AGE-GENERAL / social / social integration / social connectedness / network of friends / social support from friends / emotional support / listening to concerns
criteria_variables.mapped_leaf_embed_path: NON-CLINICAL / AGE-GENERAL / social / social integration / social connectedness / network of friends / social support from friends / emotional support / *listening to concerns
criteria_variables.mapped_rationale: Direct mapping: C01 is 'Frequency of being sought for others' problems.' The candidate leaf idx 2976 is 'listening to concerns' (external listening to others’ concerns), under social integration / social connectedness / network of friends / social support from friends / emotional support / listening to concerns. This aligns exactly with the EMA item: 'In the moment, how frequently are you approached by others to listen to or solve their problems today?' (1-9). The measurement mode is EMA_self_report on a smartphone app, 4 prompts per day, recall window since_last_prompt. Mechanistically, it captures external emotional labor load and boundary setting stress relevant to the six criteria; the pseudoprofile context emphasizes boundary overreach and suppressed distress; this item measures perceived frequency of social support requests that contribute to external emotional labor.

expected_effects.rationale: Stronger boundary setting should reduce external demands over time.
Clearer boundaries may lower difficulty in requesting help indirectly by organizing needs.
Effect on suppression is indirect.
Boundaries may reduce emotional crashes following suppression.
Less resentment when boundaries are respected.
Boundaries training should improve boundary-setting skills.

predictor_variables.risk_ethics_notes: No risky content; training tasks are safe.

feasibility.notes: Good feasibility for mobile EMA; boundary scripts are straightforward.

source_variable.label: Frequency of being sought for others' problems

measurement.item_or_signal: In the moment, how frequently are you approached by others to listen to or solve their problems today? (1-9, 1=rarely, 9=very often)
measurement.notes: Captures current external emotional labor load.

criteria.label: Difficulty requesting help
criteria.query_text_used: Difficulty requesting help | Operational item/signal: When distressed today, how difficult is it for you to ask for help or support? (1-9, 1=not difficult, 9=extremely difficult) | Polarity: higher_is_worse | Notes: Assesses personal barriers to help-seeking.

mapping.rationale: Variable label 'Difficulty requesting help' (C02) matches leaf 5057: NON-CLINICAL / AGE-GENERAL / existential / adaptability / abilitytocope / seekingsupport / *askingforhelp. It is the most specific operational construct among candidates, directly reflecting 'asking for help' as a help-seeking barrier. The EMA self-report item 'When distressed today, how difficult is it for you to ask for help or support? (1-9)' aligns with the measurement mode and item. This leaf best maps the variable to a criterion leaf with a mechanism linking distress to help-seeking behavior, suitable for within-person VAR modeling in the pseudoprofile context.

mapping.leaf_full_path: NON-CLINICAL / AGE-GENERAL / existential / adaptability / abilitytocope / seekingsupport / askingforhelp

criteria.label: Emotion suppression / masking
criteria.query_text_used: Emotion suppression / masking | Operational item/signal: Today, to what extent did you hide negative feelings behind a positive exterior? (1-9, 1=not at all, 9=very much) | Polarity: higher_is_worse | Notes: Captures habitual suppression patterns.

mapping.rationale: Variable C03 describes emotion suppression / masking measured via EMA: 'Today, to what extent did you hide negative feelings behind a positive exterior?' The closest specific CRITERION leaf is 5881 'emotion regulation in displays' (emotional awareness / emotional expression / appropriate display / emotion regulation in displays), which captures regulation of how emotions are displayed or masked in social signals. This is the most specific operational construct among candidates that aligns with suppressing or concealing negative affect in real-time reporting, compared to leaves emphasizing general emotional expression, regulation in other contexts, or broader affective processes.

measurement.item_or_signal: Today, to what extent did you hide negative feelings behind a positive exterior? (1-9, 1=not at all, 9=very much)

criteria.label: Delayed breakdowns after suppression
criteria.query_text_used: Delayed breakdowns after suppression | Operational item/signal: Since holding in feelings, have you experienced a crash or strong emotional/functional decline today? (1-9, 1=not at all, 9=very strong) | Polarity: higher_is_worse | Notes: Captures episodic declines following suppression.

mapping.rationale: Best match for Delayed breakdowns after suppression is an episodic emotional instability construct. The leaf episode_emotional_lability explicitly encodes episodic emotional lability, aligning with 'crash' or decline after suppression and with an EMA, momentary assessment. It is more specific than generic emotional_lability leaves (e.g., idx 29623) and corresponds to the pseudoprofile criterion C04 (delayed breakdowns following suppression).

measurement.item_or_signal: Since holding in feelings, have you experienced a crash or strong emotional/functional decline today? (1-9, 1=not at all, 9=very strong)

criteria.label: Role fatigue / resentment about 'being strong'
criteria.query_text_used: Role fatigue / resentment about 'being strong' | Operational item/signal: At this moment, how fatigued or resentful do you feel about being the resilient person for others? (1-9, 1=not at all, 9=very) | Polarity: higher_is_worse | Notes: Indices tolerance to ongoing resilience expectations.

mapping.rationale: Best single-leaf match for C05 given the variable's primary focus on fatigue/energy depletion from being the 'strong' helper. The leaf 5402 represents 'feeling exhausted' (subjective affective feeling), which directly corresponds to the fatigue aspect of 'Role fatigue / resentment about being the resilient person.' The measurement is EMA self-report on a 1–9 scale, with momentary assessment and four prompts per day, matching the variable's measurement mode, channel, and recall window. While the variable also mentions resentment, fatigue is the core operational construct captured by this leaf, and it aligns with the target criterion within the gVAR framework.
mapping.leaf_embed_path: NON-CLINICAL / AGE-GENERAL / emotional / subjective affective feeling / *feeling exhausted
mapping.leaf_full_path: NON-CLINICAL / AGE-GENERAL / emotional / subjective affective feeling / feeling exhausted

criteria.label: Boundary-setting and refusal skills
criteria.query_text_used: Boundary-setting and refusal skills | Operational item/signal: Today, how effectively did you set a boundary or decline a request that would reduce others’ demands? (1-9, 1=not effective, 9=very effective) | Polarity: higher_is_better | Notes: Represents real-time boundary skills.

mapping.rationale: Variable C06 corresponds to boundary-setting and refusal skills. The measurement item is EMA self-report: 'Today, how effectively did you set a boundary or decline a request that would reduce others’ demands? (1-9)'. The candidate leaf 3855 is NON-CLINICAL / AGE-GENERAL / psychological / autonomy / boundary setting / saying no when necessary / protecting personal time, which directly maps to boundary-setting and active refusal, including protecting personal time. It is the most specific leaf that captures boundary-setting and refusal behavior aligned with the EMA item.

measurement.item_or_signal: Today, how effectively did you set a boundary or decline a request that would reduce others’ demands? (1-9, 1=not effective, 9=very effective)

predictors.label: Boundary_setting_and_decline_scripts
predictors.query_text_used: Boundary_setting_and_decline_scripts | Domain: PSYCHO | Mechanism: Directly reduces external boundary overreach and improves ability to decline problematic requests, decreasing C01 load and improving targeted self-regulation. | Intervention examples: Boundary-setting scripts; decline scripts; role-clarification exercises. | Measurement: EMA_self_report | In the moment, how confidently did you set a boundary when someone asked for your time today? (1-9) | Prior ontology hint: '[PSYCHO] > Core_Psychological_Skills_Toolkits > Boundary_Setting_and_Decline_Skills'

mapping.rationale: The variable P01 is Boundary_setting_and_decline_scripts with an EMA item querying in-the-moment boundary-setting confidence. The closest and most specific leaf is boundary_setting_scripts under PSYCHO > core_psychological_skills_toolkits > interpersonal_effectiveness_and_relational_repair > assertiveness_and_boundaries > ask_say_no_negotiate > boundary_setting_scripts. This leaf directly represents boundary-setting and decline scripts (including say-no/negotiation aspects) that align with the measurement item about confidently setting a boundary when asked for time. This matches the pseudoprofile’s focus on boundary-setting as a modifiable predictor and uses a momentary, mobile EMA measurement channel consistent with the context.

source_variable.label: Boundary_setting_and_decline_scripts
source_variable.mechanism_rationale: Directly reduces external boundary overreach and improves ability to decline problematic requests, decreasing C01 load and improving targeted self-regulation.
source_variable.intervention_examples_safe: Boundary-setting scripts; decline scripts; role-clarification exercises.

measurement.item_or_signal: In the moment, how confidently did you set a boundary when someone asked for your time today? (1-9)

expected_effects.rationale: Stronger boundary setting should reduce external demands over time.
Clearer boundaries may lower difficulty in requesting help indirectly by organizing needs.
Effect on suppression is indirect.
Boundaries may reduce emotional crashes following suppression.
Less resentment when boundaries are respected.
Boundaries training should improve boundary-setting skills.

source_variable.risk_ethics_notes: No risky content; training tasks are safe.

feasibility.notes: Good feasibility for mobile EMA; boundary scripts are straightforward.

predictors.label: Assertive_help_seeking_training
predictors.query_text_used: Assertive_help_seeking_training | Domain: PSYCHO | Mechanism: Builds endogenous readiness to seek support when distressed, reducing barriers in C02 and supporting C04/C05 resilience. | Intervention examples: Communication role-plays; practice scripts for requesting support; problem-solving with supportive others. | Measurement: EMA_self_report | Today, how willing were you to ask for help when distressed? (1-9) | Prior ontology hint: '[PSYCHO] > Skills_Libraries > Assertive_Help_Seeking_Skills'

mapping.rationale: The variable P02 maps to a training-like construct focused on increasing assertiveness in help-seeking. Among candidates, the leaf 'assertiveness_training' (PSYCHO / cognitive_behavioral_therapies / cbt_skills_training_libraries / assertiveness_and_boundaries / core_skills / *assertiveness_training) is the most specific match to 'Assertive_help_seeking_training' and aligns with the EMA item 'Today, how willing were you to ask for help when distressed? (1-9)'. Mechanistically, assertiveness training builds the ability to seek support when distressed, reducing barriers (C02) and supporting resilience in C04/C05. This fits the pseudoprofile's emphasis on boundary setting, help-seeking, and emotion regulation as modifiable predictors.

source_variable.label: Assertive_help_seeking_training
source_variable.mechanism_rationale: Builds endogenous readiness to seek support when distressed, reducing barriers in C02 and supporting C04/C05 resilience.
source_variable.intervention_examples_safe: Communication role-plays; practice scripts for requesting support; problem-solving with supportive others.

measurement.item_or_signal: Today, how willing were you to ask for help when distressed? (1-9)

expected_effects.rationale: Better help-seeking can reduce external demands via clearer boundaries.
If help-seeking improves, perceived difficulty may decrease.
Expression vs suppression dynamics may vary.
Better help-seeking can reduce crashes by addressing distress earlier.
Enhanced help-seeking can reduce role fatigue by sharing load.
Assertive seeking aligns with boundary skills development.

source_variable.risk_ethics_notes: No safety concerns beyond standard privacy.

feasibility.notes: Role-play content is safe; EMA-based assessment straightforward.

predictors.label: Emotion_expression_training
predictors.query_text_used: Emotion_expression_training | Domain: PSYCHO | Mechanism: Training expressive processing reduces suppression (C03) and protects against delayed breakdowns (C04). | Intervention examples: Guided emotion labeling; expressive writing; safe venting practices. | Measurement: EMA_self_report | Today, how clearly did you express or process negative feelings in the last few hours? (1-9) | Prior ontology hint: '[PSYCHO] > Emotion_Regulation_and_Affect_Skills'

mapping.rationale: Best single match: 'art_based_emotion_expression_practice' leaf under PSYCHO > integrative_creative_and_body_oriented_interventions > creative_and_expressive_interventions. It explicitly targets emotion expression training, aligning with var label 'Emotion_expression_training' and EMA item assessing how clearly negative feelings were expressed. Other candidates focus on labeling, intensity, granularity, or tracking rather than expression; art-based expression represents a concrete, actionable expression-training approach within the same mechanism.

source_variable.label: Emotion_expression_training
source_variable.mechanism_rationale: Training expressive processing reduces suppression (C03) and protects against delayed breakdowns (C04).
source_variable.intervention_examples_safe: Guided emotion labeling; expressive writing; safe venting practices.

measurement.item_or_signal: Today, how clearly did you express or process negative feelings in the last few hours? (1-9)

expected_effects.rationale: Greater expression reduces perceived spillover of distress to others.
Expressing needs may facilitate asking for help.
Less suppression, more authentic processing.
Improved expression reduces crash risk after suppression.
Expressiveness supports resilience and reduced fatigue.
Emotion expression skills bolster boundary-friendly communication.

source_variable.risk_ethics_notes: Safe, does not involve risky activities.

feasibility.notes: Expression training feasible via app-guided tasks.

predictors.label: Self_compassion_and_resilience_training
predictors.query_text_used: Self_compassion_and_resilience_training | Domain: PSYCHO | Mechanism: Fosters adaptive self-relating, reducing self-criticism and avoidance, with downstream benefits for all criteria (esp. C04 and C05). | Intervention examples: Loving-kindness practice; compassionate self-talk; resilience journaling. | Measurement: EMA_self_report | Today, how kind and supportive were you to yourself in responding to distress? (1-9) | Prior ontology hint: '[PSYCHO] > Core_Psychological_Skills_Toolkits > Self_Compassion_and_Affective_Mastery'

mapping.rationale: Best-matching leaf: PSYCHO / core_psychological_skills_toolkits / compassionate_self_relating_and_inner_support / self_compassion_practices / warmth_and_common_humanity / self_compassion_break_practice. Rationale: The variable P04 corresponds to Self_compassion_and_resilience_training. The EMA item 'Today, how kind and supportive were you to yourself in responding to distress? (1-9)' directly maps to self-compassion practices. This leaf aligns with the ontology_path [PSYCHO] > Core_Psychological_Skills_Toolkits > Self_Compassion_and_Affective_Mastery, specifically the self_compassion_practices node within compassionate_self_relating_and_inner_support. Mechanism: self-compassion practices foster adaptive self-relating, reduce self-criticism, and enhance affect regulation, yielding downstream benefits for all six criteria (notably C04 and C05) while remaining a modular, trainable predictor in the gVAR framework. Pseudoprofile context supports using this precise, momentary self-compassion construct as a core, modifiable lever.

source_variable.label: Self_compassion_and_resilience_training
source_variable.mechanism_rationale: Fosters adaptive self-relating, reducing self-criticism and avoidance, with downstream benefits for all criteria (esp. C04 and C05).
source_variable.intervention_examples_safe: Loving-kindness practice; compassionate self-talk; resilience journaling.

measurement.item_or_signal: Today, how kind and supportive were you to yourself in responding to distress? (1-9)

expected_effects.rationale: Self-compassion may buffer external demands' impact.
Kind self-talk can lower perceived difficulty in seeking help.
Expression suppression decreases with self-compassion.
Reduced crash risk via kinder self-relating.
Resilience building supports sustained functioning.
Self-compassion enhances boundary-appropriate responses.

feasibility.notes: Can be delivered via app-guided micro-interventions.

predictors.label: Sleep_circadian_optimization_and_awareness
predictors.query_text_used: Sleep_circadian_optimization_and_awareness | Domain: BIO | Mechanism: Biological regulation of arousal/energy supports all psychosocial regulation processes and may reduce crash risk (C04) and fatigue (C05). | Intervention examples: Light-schedule alignment; regular sleep window; avoid late caffeine. | Measurement: passive_phone_sensor | Sleep duration last night (hours) | Prior ontology hint: '[BIO] > Sleep_and_Circadian > Circadian_Entraining_Solutions'

mapping.rationale: P05 Sleep_circadian_optimization_and_awareness targets biological regulation of sleep/energy to support psychosocial regulation. The measurement for this variable is a passive daily aggregate of Sleep duration last night (hours). The most specific ontology leaf that directly corresponds to a passive, objective sleep measurement device is the actigraphy device leaf: BIO / sleep_circadian_and_restoration / sleep_devices_and_technologies / tracking_and_feedback / actigraphy_device (idx=6287). This leaf embodies the hardware/technology used to capture sleep duration data passively, aligning with the measurement modality and the circadian entrainment goal. Other circadian leaves primarily address cues, lighting, diaries, or interventions, which are less precisely tied to a passive sleep-duration signal.

source_variable.label: Sleep_circadian_optimization_and_awareness
source_variable.mechanism_rationale: Biological regulation of arousal/energy supports all psychosocial regulation processes and may reduce crash risk (C04) and fatigue (C05).
source_variable.intervention_examples_safe: Light-schedule alignment; regular sleep window; avoid late caffeine.

measurement.item_or_signal: Sleep duration last night (hours)

expected_effects.rationale: Better sleep may reduce external demands impact by improving energy.
Rested individuals more able to seek help when distressed.
Improved sleep supports emotion processing.
Sleep quality reduces crash risk after suppression.
Energy reserves reduce fatigue/resentment.
Good sleep supports boundary-appropriate communication.

source_variable.risk_ethics_notes: No risk; uses standard sleep-tracking data.

feasibility.notes: Requires compatible sleep-tracking device or app.

predictors.label: Mindfulness_and_ACT_based_regulation
predictors.query_text_used: Mindfulness_and_ACT_based_regulation | Domain: PSYCHO | Mechanism: Enhances present-mocused awareness and nonreactivity to distress, supporting multiple criteria via improved emotion regulation and reduced avoidance. | Intervention examples: Brief MBCT/MBRS practices; ACT-based distress tolerance; mindful check-ins. | Measurement: EMA_self_report | In the last few hours, how mindful and present were you to your emotions? (1-9) | Prior ontology hint: '[PSYCHO] > Third_Wave_and_Contextual_Therapies > Mindfulness_Based_Interventions'

mapping.rationale: Best match for Mindfulness_and_ACT_based_regulation: leaf under ACT-contextual therapy capturing present-moment emotional mindfulness. Variable measurement asks how mindful and present one is to emotions (last few hours). The candidate leaf mindfulness_of_emotions under act_contextual_behavioral_therapy > present_moment_awareness encodes present-moment awareness of emotions within an ACT framework, matching the intended mechanism (present-focused awareness and nonreactivity to distress). This is more specific to ACT-based present-moment regulation than other mindfulness leaves that target thoughts or generic body/mindfulness skills.

source_variable.label: Mindfulness_and_ACT_based_regulation
source_variable.mechanism_rationale: Enhances present-mocused awareness and nonreactivity to distress, supporting multiple criteria via improved emotion regulation and reduced avoidance.
source_variable.intervention_examples_safe: Brief MBCT/MBRS practices; ACT-based distress tolerance; mindful check-ins.

measurement.item_or_signal: In the last few hours, how mindful and present were you to your emotions? (1-9)

expected_effects.rationale: Increased mindful awareness may reduce perceived external demands by prioritizing needs.
Mindfulness supports clearer help-seeking decisions.
Nonjudgmental awareness reduces suppression.
Emotion processing improves, reducing breakdowns.
Mindfulness supports energy and resilience.
Mindfulness improves interpersonal communication and boundary setting.

feasibility.notes: Requires engagement with brief mindfulness tasks.
